lines.line: > The speaker, Bruno Pizarro, is the director of brand strategy at a cultural and design brand strategy studio called 'for La diaspora,' which they launched with their husband. The studio aims to create visual communications tailored to the Latin community, drawing on the speaker's experiences during their tenure as the executive director at Junta for Progressive Action, especially during the pandemic.

metadata.title: Current Role and Project
metadata.synopsis: The speaker, Bruno Pizarro, is the director of brand strategy at a cultural and design brand strategy studio called 'for La diaspora,' which they launched with their husband. The studio aims to create visual communications tailored to the Latin community, drawing on the speaker's experiences during their tenure as the executive director at Junta for Progressive Action, especially during the pandemic.

lines.line: # Background and Connection to Puerto Rico

> The speaker grew up in New York City, with their father having migrated from Puerto Rico at the age of two. Their mother also has Puerto Rican roots, and after their parents married, they were raised in Queens, New York. The speaker's connection to Puerto Rico is through their family's migratory history.

metadata.title: Background and Connection to Puerto Rico
metadata.synopsis: The speaker grew up in New York City, with their father having migrated from Puerto Rico at the age of two. Their mother also has Puerto Rican roots, and after their parents married, they were raised in Queens, New York. The speaker's connection to Puerto Rico is through their family's migratory history.

lines.line: > The speaker's educational journey took them from New York City to Vanderbilt University in Nashville, Tennessee, and then to Aboriginal Australia. These experiences, particularly the stark contrast between life in the North and the South of the United States and the health outcomes in Aboriginal communities, shaped their understanding of medicine, identity, and environmental justice. This led to a shift in career focus from medicine to environmental science.

metadata.title: Educational Journey and Personal Growth
metadata.synopsis: The speaker's educational journey took them from New York City to Vanderbilt University in Nashville, Tennessee, and then to Aboriginal Australia. These experiences, particularly the stark contrast between life in the North and the South of the United States and the health outcomes in Aboriginal communities, shaped their understanding of medicine, identity, and environmental justice. This led to a shift in career focus from medicine to environmental science.

lines.line: > The speaker's work at Junta for Progressive Action involved resettling families from Puerto Rico post-Hurricane Maria and Irma. They focused on capturing the history and experiences of these families, particularly from a gendered perspective. The organization, founded by Puerto Rican organizers, provided a sense of community and support for Latin American immigrants in New Haven.

metadata.title: Work Experience and Community Impact
metadata.synopsis: The speaker's work at Junta for Progressive Action involved resettling families from Puerto Rico post-Hurricane Maria and Irma. They focused on capturing the history and experiences of these families, particularly from a gendered perspective. The organization, founded by Puerto Rican organizers, provided a sense of community and support for Latin American immigrants in New Haven.

lines.line: > The speaker emphasizes the importance of listening to the community's needs and being transparent about organizational goals. They describe how during the pandemic, they adapted their strategies to engage the community by hosting pop-up clinics with cultural entertainment, which proved to be more effective than traditional methods.

metadata.title: Community Engagement and Civic Participation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker emphasizes the importance of listening to the community's needs and being transparent about organizational goals. They describe how during the pandemic, they adapted their strategies to engage the community by hosting pop-up clinics with cultural entertainment, which proved to be more effective than traditional methods.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the importance of honesty and transparency in coalition building, emphasizing the need to focus on community needs rather than individual organizational goals. Trust is highlighted as a fundamental aspect of successful coalition building, and the speaker suggests that recognizing and utilizing the assets within communities is key to fostering unity.

metadata.title: Coalition Building Among Different Groups
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the importance of honesty and transparency in coalition building, emphasizing the need to focus on community needs rather than individual organizational goals. Trust is highlighted as a fundamental aspect of successful coalition building, and the speaker suggests that recognizing and utilizing the assets within communities is key to fostering unity.

lines.line: # Vision for Puerto Rican Community Improvement

> The speaker believes that the Puerto Rican community needs to focus on recognizing and utilizing their individual and collective assets. They stress the importance of resourcefulness and community bonding, suggesting that a stronger cultural fabric can be built through mutual support and celebration of cultural diversity.

They stress the importance of resourcefulness and community bonding, suggesting that a stronger cultural fabric can be built through mutual support and celebration of cultural diversity.

lines.line: > The speaker is working on projects that involve visual communications and storytelling, aiming to connect with the Latin community through culturally relevant visual icons. They believe in the power of storytelling to create connections and build trust, and they are working with their husband in a design studio to bring these elements together for community engagement.

# Background and Connection to Puerto Rico

> The speaker grew up in New York City, with their father having migrated from Puerto Rico at the age of two. Their mother also has Puerto Rican roots, and after their parents married, they were raised in Queens, New York. The speaker's connection to Puerto Rico is through their family's migratory history.

> The speaker's educational journey took them from New York City to Vanderbilt University in Nashville, Tennessee, and then to Aboriginal Australia. These experiences, particularly the stark contrast between life in the North and the South of the United States and the health outcomes in Aboriginal communities, shaped their understanding of medicine, identity, and environmental justice. This led to a shift in career focus from medicine to environmental science.

> The speaker's work at Junta for Progressive Action involved resettling families from Puerto Rico post-Hurricane Maria and Irma. They focused on capturing the history and experiences of these families, particularly from a gendered perspective. The organization, founded by Puerto Rican organizers, provided a sense of community and support for Latin American immigrants in New Haven.

> The speaker emphasizes the importance of listening to the community's needs and being transparent about organizational goals. Trust is highlighted as a fundamental aspect of successful coalition building, and the speaker suggests that recognizing and utilizing the assets within communities is key to fostering unity.
# Vision for Puerto Rican Community Improvement

> The speaker believes that the Puerto Rican community needs to focus on recognizing and utilizing their individual and collective assets. They stress the importance of resourcefulness and community bonding, suggesting that a stronger cultural fabric can be built through mutual support and celebration of cultural diversity.

> The speaker is working on projects that involve visual communications and storytelling, aiming to connect with the Latin community through culturally relevant visual icons. They believe in the power of storytelling to create connections and build trust, and they are working with their husband in a design studio to bring these elements together for community engagement.

story.title: Briuni Pizarro
story.description: The interview provides an overview of Bruno Pizarro's journey from his upbringing in New York City to his role as a director of brand strategy. Pizarro discusses his cultural heritage as a diasporic Puerto Rican and his experiences at Vanderbilt University and in Aboriginal Australia. He reflects on his involvement with environmental justice and community engagement, particularly during his tenure at Junta for Progressive Action, where he worked to support Puerto Rican families in New Haven post-Hurricane Maria. The conversation highlights strategies for civic engagement, such as leveraging cultural nuances and community assets, coalition building across diverse groups, and the importance of visual communication to engage the Latin community effectively.
